title: The case for letting some neighbourhoods grow old
summary: Defend the idea that not every district needs renovation, gentrification, or polish.
topic: The case for letting some neighbourhoods grow old
prompt: Write an essay (350–500 words) defending the position that some neighbourhoods should be allowed to grow old without renovation. Use a concession, a cleft sentence, and at least one international example (Naples' Sanità, Lisbon's Mouraria, or similar).
hintStarters: There is a stretch of the Sanità district in Naples…
What renovation often removes, without naming it, is…
Granted, ageing buildings carry real costs.
It may well be that some neighbourhoods…

hintVocab.meaningVi: quá trình thượng lưu hóa khu phố
bị thời tiết bào mòn (động từ)
sự bị đẩy ra khỏi nơi ở
bị đẩy ra do giá tăng

sampleText:
There is a stretch of the Sanità district in Naples where the walls have not been painted in forty years. Plaster peels in slow arcs above the bread shops. Laundry crosses the street on lines so old that the wood pulleys have been smoothed by hands rather than tools. Visitors who come from cleaner cities sometimes feel they have stepped into a film set, and they tend to ask the same question: why has no one fixed it? The answer is more interesting than the question. Some of it has been fixed. Most of it has been left, on purpose.

What renovation often removes, without naming it, is the slow accumulation of habit. A patina is not just dirt; it is the shape that hands and shoulders and ladders and bicycles have pressed into a building over decades. Scrub it off and you have a cleaner wall and a thinner street. The shopkeeper who used to lean against that wall, smoking between customers, may still lean — but he will lean against something that no longer remembers him.

Granted, ageing buildings carry real costs. Damp climbs into ceilings; wiring becomes a fire risk; an unfixed staircase can break an old woman's hip. No serious defender of slow neighbourhoods denies this. The argument is not for neglect but for selective restraint: fix the wiring, leave the façade; fix the staircase, leave the courtyard; fix what kills, leave what only ages. The opposite — renovating everything to a single polished standard — does not just change buildings, it changes who can afford to live near them.

What's more, the most loved districts in many cities are precisely the ones that escaped a comprehensive plan. Lisbon's Mouraria, Istanbul's Balat, parts of Mexico City's Roma Sur — these neighbourhoods are loved not because they are pretty by design, but because they are slightly wrong in a hundred small ways that no architect would have permitted. Their rough edges are also the reason rents stayed low long enough for grandmothers, immigrants, and small workshops to remain.

It may well be that some neighbourhoods need to be allowed to grow old the way people do — supported where they hurt, but not erased where they show their age. A city that scrubs every wall clean ends up looking like every other scrubbed city, and the residents who gave it character get priced out of the postcard they were standing in. Some streets earn the right to peel.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft naming what is removed
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What renovation often removes, without naming it, is the slow accumulation of habit' đặt khái niệm ở cuối câu — đó là kỹ thuật điển hình để định nghĩa lại một thứ mà người đọc tưởng đã biết.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What X removes is Y' is a what-cleft that delays the key concept (Y) to the end, redefining something the reader already thought they understood.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Hedged claim with 'may well be that'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It may well be that some neighbourhoods need to be allowed to grow old' dùng 'may well be that' để đưa ra một kết luận thận trọng — phù hợp khi viết argumentative ở B2.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It may well be that + clause' is a hedged way of stating a conclusion you believe but don't want to assert as fact — ideal for B2 argumentative writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một đoạn ở quận Sanità tại Naples nơi tường đã không được sơn lại suốt bốn mươi năm. Lớp vữa bong tróc thành những cung chậm phía trên các tiệm bánh mì. Quần áo phơi vắt ngang phố trên những sợi dây cũ đến mức ròng rọc gỗ đã được làm nhẵn bởi bàn tay chứ không phải dụng cụ. Du khách đến từ những thành phố sạch sẽ hơn đôi khi cảm thấy như họ bước vào phim trường, và họ thường hỏi cùng một câu hỏi: tại sao không ai sửa nó? Câu trả lời thú vị hơn câu hỏi. Một số đã được sửa. Phần lớn đã được để lại, một cách có chủ ý.

Điều mà việc cải tạo thường loại bỏ, mà không gọi tên ra, là sự tích lũy chậm của thói quen. Lớp bóng thời gian không chỉ là bụi bẩn; nó là hình dạng mà bàn tay, vai, thang, và xe đạp đã ép vào một tòa nhà qua nhiều thập kỷ. Cọ nó đi và bạn có một bức tường sạch hơn và một con phố mỏng hơn. Người chủ tiệm thường tựa vào bức tường đó, hút thuốc giữa các khách hàng, có thể vẫn tựa — nhưng ông sẽ tựa vào thứ không còn nhớ ông nữa.

Công nhận, các tòa nhà cũ mang theo những chi phí thật. Ẩm thấp leo vào trần; hệ thống điện trở thành nguy cơ cháy; một cầu thang chưa sửa có thể làm gãy hông một bà cụ. Không người bảo vệ nghiêm túc nào của các khu phố chậm phủ nhận điều này. Lập luận không phải là bỏ mặc mà là kiềm chế có chọn lọc: sửa hệ thống điện, để lại mặt tiền; sửa cầu thang, để lại sân trong; sửa những gì giết người, để lại những gì chỉ già đi. Ngược lại — cải tạo tất cả theo một tiêu chuẩn bóng bẩy duy nhất — không chỉ thay đổi các tòa nhà, nó thay đổi cả những người có thể đủ tiền sống gần chúng.

Hơn nữa, những khu phố được yêu thích nhất ở nhiều thành phố chính là những khu thoát khỏi một kế hoạch toàn diện. Mouraria của Lisbon, Balat của Istanbul, một phần của Roma Sur ở Mexico City — những khu phố này được yêu không phải vì đẹp theo thiết kế, mà vì chúng hơi sai theo cả trăm cách nhỏ mà không kiến trúc sư nào sẽ cho phép. Những góc cạnh thô ráp của chúng cũng là lý do giá thuê đủ rẻ đủ lâu để các bà, người nhập cư, và xưởng nhỏ vẫn ở lại.

Rất có thể là một số khu phố cần được phép già đi theo cách con người vẫn già — được hỗ trợ ở chỗ chúng đau, nhưng không bị xóa nơi chúng cho thấy tuổi tác. Một thành phố lau chùi sạch mọi bức tường cuối cùng nhìn giống như mọi thành phố được lau chùi khác, và những cư dân tạo nên tính cách của nó bị đẩy ra khỏi tấm bưu thiếp mà họ đang đứng. Một số con phố giành được quyền được bong tróc.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which what-cleft most naturally redefines what renovation does?
options: What renovation often removes, without naming it, is the slow accumulation of habit.
Renovation often what removes is slow accumulation without naming.
It is what renovation removes that habit slow accumulation often.
Renovation removes what is the slow without naming accumulation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X removes is Y' delays the key noun for emphasis.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses concession most naturally?
options: Granted, ageing buildings carry real costs.
Of course granted ageing carry real costs buildings.
Conceding granted that the buildings ageing real costs they.
Although granted ageing buildings real costs carry which.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, + clause' is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick 'Granted, …' followed by a fluent main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive most naturally for topic-fronting?
options: Most of it has been left, on purpose.
Most it left have been by purpose.
Most been has left it on purpose of.
On purpose of most being left it has been.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Most of it has been left, on purpose' uses present-perfect passive cleanly.

options: to scrub clean a wall
to scrub patina a delivery
to scrub renovate a courtyard
to scrub displacement a façade
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Scrub clean' is the natural verb-adjective collocation for thoroughly cleaning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces an additional argument most naturally?
options: Whatever moreover, the most loved districts escaped a plan.
What's more, the most loved districts in many cities are precisely the ones that escaped a comprehensive plan.
In conclusion, the districts most loved escaped a plan.
Throughout history, all loved districts escape plans.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What's more, …' is a clean cohesive marker that adds a related, specific claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses hedged generalisation correctly?
options: It may well be that some neighbourhoods need to be allowed to grow old the way people do.
Some neighbourhoods may well grow old people the way do.
It well may that the some grow neighbourhood old way.
May well some neighbourhood grow old people the way.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It may well be that + clause' is the right hedged-conclusion structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It may well be that + subject + verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best continues the previous sentence ending '…thinner street'?
options: Throughout history streets have been thin.
It is important to note that walls can be cleaned.
The shopkeeper who used to lean against that wall, smoking between customers, may still lean — but he will lean against something that no longer remembers him.
In conclusion, dirty walls are good.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C develops the image — the wall losing memory of those who touched it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option extends the image rather than restating a generality?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses participle-clause syntax correctly?
options: Visitors who come from cleaner cities sometimes feel they have stepped into a film set.
Visitors coming from cleaner cities feeling sometimes have stepped a film set.
Coming visitors from cleaner cities feel having stepped film set.
From cleaner cities visitors coming feel film set stepping.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Visitors who come from cleaner cities…' uses a defining relative clause cleanly.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option with a clean 'who' clause.

options: Something that no longer remembers him.
Something no longer that remembers him.
Something he remembers no longer that.
Him remembers no longer something that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No longer + verb' goes between subject and lexical verb (auxiliary if present).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the natural word order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which ending avoids preaching and stays inside the argument?
options: We must all respect old buildings as a moral duty.
In conclusion, neighbourhoods are important and we should preserve them.

A city that scrubs every wall clean ends up looking like every other scrubbed city, and the residents who gave it character get priced out of the postcard they were standing in. Some streets earn the right to peel.
Throughout history, all cities have struggled with this question.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C ends with concrete image and ironic compression rather than a moral lesson.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the ending that ends with an image, not a sermon.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'precisely' correctly to sharpen a claim?
options: Precisely the districts loved escaped a plan most.
The most loved districts in many cities are precisely the ones that escaped a comprehensive plan.
Loved most districts precisely escaped a plan that.
Are precisely the most loved a plan districts escape.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Precisely + the/this + noun' emphasises the exact identity of what's being claimed.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which subtle cohesion choice keeps the paragraph specific rather than abstract?
options: These neighbourhoods are loved for many reasons we don't always know.
These neighbourhoods are loved not because they are pretty by design, but because they are slightly wrong in a hundred small ways that no architect would have permitted.
These neighbourhoods are generally just nicer than other ones in modern times.
These neighbourhoods are loved due to many factors and many reasons.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B keeps the argument specific with a concrete contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence names what it's claiming?

options: fire risk
fire patina
fire footfall
fire shopfront
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Fire risk' is a standard noun-noun collocation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure correctly?
options: Fix the wiring, leave the façade; fix the staircase, leave the courtyard; fix what kills, leave what only ages.
Fix wiring, leaving façade; fixed staircase, courtyard leave; fix kills, leaving ages.
Fixing the wiring left the façade; fixing the staircase left the courtyard; fixing kill left ages.
Wired fix, façade leave; staircase fix, courtyard leaving; what kills fix, what ages leave.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A uses parallel imperatives ('fix X, leave Y') across three pairs.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'tend to' correctly to hedge a generalisation about visitors?
options: Visitors who come from cleaner cities tend to ask the same question.
Tend visitors from cleaner cities ask the same question they.
Visitors tend cleaner cities to ask the question same from.
Tend to the same question visitors cleaner cities.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Subject + tend to + base verb' is the standard hedged-generalisation form.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of one neighbourhood you know personally. If a planner gave you a small budget — enough to fix what kills but not what ages — what would you fix, and what would you leave?
